Test 55: Can your write a chess bot? (C++) Score: 0.00 / 40
Typical Prompt:
Write a C++ chess bot that for any given chess position, returns the best move to make (or the best
move you can find before the timeout).

You receive the chess position as a string in FEN notation, and you must return the move in
disambiguated algebraic notation (including the starting square, piece type, and destination square).

See:
- https://www.chess.com/terms/fen-chess

Here is an example FEN representing the starting position with white to move:
```
rnbqkbnr/pppppppp/8/8/8/8/PPPPPPPP/RNBQKBNR w KQkq - 0 1
```

Here is an example disambiguated algebraic move:
```
pe2e4
```
This represents the king's pawn opening - moving the pawn from e2 to e4.

**Move notation rules:**
- Piece letter (lowercase p for pawn, uppercase N/B/R/Q/K for others) + source square + destination square.
- Use "x" between source and destination for captures: `pe4xd5`
- Promotion: append `/` and the piece letter, e.g. `pe7e8/Q`
- Castling: `O-O` (kingside) or `O-O-O` (queenside).
- En passant: append `ep`, e.g. `pe5xd6ep`
- Check: append `+`, e.g. `Bb4c5+`
- Checkmate: append `#`, e.g. `Qd1h5#`

**Input format (stdin, two lines):**
```
timeout_in_seconds
fen_string
```

**Output format (stdout, one line):**
```
move_in_disambiguated_algebraic
```

timeout_in_seconds will be at least 5. Your code will be compiled with full optimisations and
run on a machine with multiple cores, so threading is encouraged.

**Environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: Intel64 Family 6 Model 158 Stepping 10, GenuineIntel
Core count: 12
Memory: 31.93 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3
SIMD detail: AVX=yes, AVX-512F=no, AVX2=yes, SSE=yes, SSE2=yes, SSE3=yes, SSE4.1=yes, SSE4.2=yes, SSSE3=yes

**C++ Compiler:**
MSVC cl 19.50.35728 | std=c++20 (/std:c++20)

Be sure that any deviation from the C++ standard library is supported by the given compiler,
as referencing the wrong intrinsics or non-standard header (like 'bits/stdc++.h') could fail your submission.

Write complete, compilable C++ code with a main() function.
 Prompt Changes:
Prompt remains constant
Subpass 0
Parameters: Fool's mate - Qh4#
View exact prompt
View raw AI output
View chain of thought

Write a C++ chess engine that, given any board position in FEN notation, returns the best move it can find within a time limit. The engine is compiled once and then called repeatedly — once per move — as it faces increasingly difficult challenges.

Early subpasses test trivial tactics (hanging pieces, mate in one). Middle subpasses present classic chess puzzles requiring short tactical sequences. Later subpasses pit the engine against Stockfish at rising ELO levels, from absolute beginner (100) to grandmaster strength (2500).

Test 56: Delivery Route Planner (JavaScript) Score: 13.99 / 14
Typical Prompt:
Write a complete Node.js JavaScript program for this benchmark task.

Plan delivery routes for a fleet of vans.

The input JSON contains:
- `depot`: [x, y]
- `truckCount`: maximum number of vans/routes
- `packages`: objects `{id, x, y}`

Return JSON:
```
{"routes": [[packageId, packageId, ...], ...]}
```
Every package must appear exactly once. Each route starts and ends at the depot.
The score compares your total distance to a deterministic reference planner.

**Input format:**
The program receives one JSON document on stdin. Use this pattern to read it:
```
const fs = require('fs'); const input = fs.readFileSync(0, 'utf8');
const data = JSON.parse(input);
```

**Output format:**
Print one JSON document to stdout. Do not print logs or explanatory text.

**Runtime environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: Intel64 Family 6 Model 158 Stepping 10, GenuineIntel
Core count: 12
Memory: 31.93 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3
SIMD detail: AVX=yes, AVX-512F=no, AVX2=yes, SSE=yes, SSE2=yes, SSE3=yes, SSE4.1=yes, SSE4.2=yes, SSSE3=yes
Node.js: v24.14.0

The grader will run the same program on increasingly large scenarios. Use algorithms
that scale; avoid browser APIs, network access, and external npm dependencies.
Return the complete runnable JavaScript source code.
 Prompt Changes:

Subpass 0
Parameters: (see typical prompt)View exact prompt
View raw AI output
View chain of thought
Plan multi-vehicle delivery routes over clustered city coordinates.		 1.0000
[30 packages, 2 vans] distance 3027.0, reference 3027.0, score 1.000 (run 0.06s)

Delivery Route Planner - 30 packages, 2 vans

View JavaScript code (1656 chars)
const fs = require('fs');

function solve() {
  const input = fs.readFileSync(0, 'utf8');
  if (!input) return;
  const data = JSON.parse(input);
  const { depot, truckCount, packages } = data;

  if (packages.length === 0) {
    console.log(JSON.stringify({ routes: [] }));
    return;
  }

  // Calculate angle for each package relative to the depot
  const packagesWithAngle = packages.map(pkg => ({
    ...pkg,
    angle: Math.atan2(pkg.y - depot[1], pkg.x - depot[0])
  }));

  // Sort by angle to enable clustering
  packagesWithAngle.sort((a, b) => a.angle - b.angle);

  // Divide packages into trucks
  const routes = [];
  const chunkSize = Math.ceil(packagesWithAngle.length / truckCount);

  for (let i = 0; i < truckCount; i++) {
    const chunk = packagesWithAngle.slice(i * chunkSize, (i + 1) * chunkSize);
    if (chunk.length === 0) continue;

    // Nearest Neighbor for each truck
    const route = [];
    let currentPos = { x: depot[0], y: depot[1] };
    const unvisited = [...chunk];

    while (unvisited.length > 0) {
      let nearestIndex = -1;
      let minDistance = Infinity;

      for (let j = 0; j < unvisited.length; j++) {
        const pkg = unvisited[j];
        const dist = Math.sqrt(
          Math.pow(pkg.x - currentPos.x, 2) + Math.pow(pkg.y - currentPos.y, 2)
        );
        if (dist < minDistance) {
          minDistance = dist;
          nearestIndex = j;
        }
      }

      const nextPkg = unvisited.splice(nearestIndex, 1)[0];
      route.push(nextPkg.id);
      currentPos = { x: nextPkg.x, y: nextPkg.y };
    }
    routes.push(route);
  }

  console.log(JSON.stringify({ routes }));
}

solve();

Test 57: Wildfire Firebreak Planner (JavaScript) Score: 0.00 / 7
Typical Prompt:
Write a complete Node.js JavaScript program for this benchmark task.

Choose where to cut firebreaks before a wildfire spreads.

The input JSON contains:
- `width`, `height`
- `grid`: strings where `.` is burnable forest and `~` is water/rock
- `ignitions`: starting fire cells `[x, y]`
- `towns`: objects `{id, x, y, value}`
- `cutBudget`: maximum number of forest cells you may cut

Fire spreads in four directions through burnable cells. Water/rock and your cut
cells block fire. Return JSON:
```
{"firebreaks": [[x, y], [x, y], ...]}
```
The score is the fraction of otherwise-burned town value saved.

**Input format:**
The program receives one JSON document on stdin. Use this pattern to read it:
```
const fs = require('fs'); const input = fs.readFileSync(0, 'utf8');
const data = JSON.parse(input);
```

**Output format:**
Print one JSON document to stdout. Do not print logs or explanatory text.

**Runtime environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: Intel64 Family 6 Model 158 Stepping 10, GenuineIntel
Core count: 12
Memory: 31.93 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3
SIMD detail: AVX=yes, AVX-512F=no, AVX2=yes, SSE=yes, SSE2=yes, SSE3=yes, SSE4.1=yes, SSE4.2=yes, SSSE3=yes
Node.js: v24.14.0

The grader will run the same program on increasingly large scenarios. Use algorithms
that scale; avoid browser APIs, network access, and external npm dependencies.
Return the complete runnable JavaScript source code.
 Prompt Changes:
Prompt parameters change between subpasses (increasing difficulty)
Subpass 0
Parameters: (see typical prompt)View exact prompt
View raw AI output
View chain of thought
Choose firebreak cells on a forest map to protect towns from spreading fire.		 0.0000
[32x22 forest, 3 towns] town value burned 27/27, burned cells 653/663, valid cuts 10 (run 0.06s)

Wildfire Firebreak Planner - 32x22 forest, 3 towns

View JavaScript code (2693 chars)
const fs = require('fs');

function solve() {
  const input = fs.readFileSync(0, 'utf8');
  const data = JSON.parse(input);
  const { width, height, grid, ignitions, towns, cutBudget } = data;

  // 1. Map grid to a 2D array for easier access
  const map = grid.map(row => row.split(''));

  // 2. Find paths from ignitions to all cells
  // Use BFS to find the shortest distance from any ignition point to all other cells
  const dist = Array.from({ length: height }, () => Array(width).fill(Infinity));
  const queue = [];

  for (const [x, y] of ignitions) {
    if (map[y][x] === '.') {
      dist[y][x] = 0;
      queue.push([x, y]);
    }
  }

  let head = 0;
  while (head < queue.length) {
    const [x, y] = queue[head++];
    const d = dist[y][x];

    const dirs = [[0, 1], [0, -1], [1, 0], [-1, 0]];
    for (const [dx, dy] of dirs) {
      const nx = x + dx;
      const ny = y + dy;
      if (nx >= 0 && nx < width && ny >= 0 && ny < height && map[ny][nx] === '.' && dist[ny][nx] === Infinity) {
        dist[ny][nx] = d + 1;
        queue.push([nx, ny]);
      }
    }
  }

  // 3. Identify potential firebreak locations
  // Count how many high-value towns are affected by each burnable cell.
  // This is a simple heuristic: if a cell is on a path to a town, it's a candidate.
  const candidates = new Map();

  for (const town of towns) {
    const { x, y, value } = town;
    if (dist[y][x] === Infinity) continue; // Already blocked or unreachable

    // Backtrack from town to ignition to find the path
    let currX = x;
    let currY = y;
    while (dist[currY][currX] > 0) {
      let nextX = currX;
      let nextY = currY;
      let minD = dist[currY][currX];

      const dirs = [[0, 1], [0, -1], [1, 0], [-1, 0]];
      for (const [dx, dy] of dirs) {
        const nx = currX + dx;
        const ny = currY + dy;
        if (nx >= 0 && nx < width && ny >= 0 && ny < height && dist[ny][nx] < minD) {
          minD = dist[ny][nx];
          nextX = nx;
          nextY = ny;
        }
      }
      currX = nextX;
      currY = nextY;

      // Add as candidate if it's not the starting point
      if (dist[currY][currX] > 0) {
        const key = `${currX},${currY}`;
        candidates.set(key, (candidates.get(key) || 0) + value);
      }
    }
  }

  // 4. Select the best firebreaks
  const sortedCandidates = Array.from(candidates.entries())
    .sort((a, b) => b[1] - a[1]);

  const firebreaks = [];
  for (let i = 0; i < Math.min(cutBudget, sortedCandidates.length); i++) {
    const [coords] = sortedCandidates[i];
    firebreaks.push(coords.split(',').map(Number));
  }

  // 5. Output
  process.stdout.write(JSON.stringify({ firebreaks }));
}

solve();

Test 58: Warehouse Robot Job Planner (JavaScript) Score: 3.00 / 6
Typical Prompt:
Write a complete Node.js JavaScript program for this benchmark task.

Assign warehouse pick jobs to robots.

The input JSON contains:
- `grid`: strings where `.` is open floor and `#` is blocked shelving
- `robots`: objects `{id, x, y}`
- `jobs`: objects `{id, x, y, priority}`

Return JSON:
```
{"assignments": [[jobId, jobId, ...], ...]}
```
The array index is the robot id. Every job must appear exactly once. The grader
uses shortest grid walking distance through open cells and compares total travel
to a deterministic greedy reference.

**Input format:**
The program receives one JSON document on stdin. Use this pattern to read it:
```
const fs = require('fs'); const input = fs.readFileSync(0, 'utf8');
const data = JSON.parse(input);
```

**Output format:**
Print one JSON document to stdout. Do not print logs or explanatory text.

**Runtime environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: Intel64 Family 6 Model 158 Stepping 10, GenuineIntel
Core count: 12
Memory: 31.93 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3
SIMD detail: AVX=yes, AVX-512F=no, AVX2=yes, SSE=yes, SSE2=yes, SSE3=yes, SSE4.1=yes, SSE4.2=yes, SSSE3=yes
Node.js: v24.14.0

The grader will run the same program on increasingly large scenarios. Use algorithms
that scale; avoid browser APIs, network access, and external npm dependencies.
Return the complete runnable JavaScript source code.
 Prompt Changes:
Prompt parameters change between subpasses (increasing difficulty)
Subpass 0
Parameters: (see typical prompt)View exact prompt
View raw AI output
View chain of thought
Assign warehouse pick jobs to robots moving through aisles and blocked shelves.		 1.0000
[28 jobs, 2 robots] travel 137, reference 137, score 1.000 (run 0.12s)

Warehouse Robot Job Planner - 28 jobs, 2 robots

View JavaScript code (2668 chars)
const fs = require('fs');

function solve() {
    const input = fs.readFileSync(0, 'utf8');
    const { grid, robots, jobs } = JSON.parse(input);

    const robotPositions = {};
    const assignments = {};
    const robotIds = robots.map(r => r.id);
    const maxRobotId = Math.max(...robotIds);
    
    // Initialize assignments and robot positions
    for (const robot of robots) {
        robotPositions[robot.id] = { x: robot.x, y: robot.y };
        assignments[robot.id] = [];
    }

    const distanceCache = new Map();

    function getDistance(start, end) {
        const key = `${start.x},${start.y}->${end.x},${end.y}`;
        if (distanceCache.has(key)) return distanceCache.get(key);
        
        const queue = [[start.x, start.y, 0]];
        const visited = new Set();
        visited.add(`${start.x},${start.y}`);
        
        while (queue.length > 0) {
            const [x, y, dist] = queue.shift();
            if (x === end.x && y === end.y) {
                distanceCache.set(key, dist);
                return dist;
            }
            const neighbors = [[x + 1, y], [x - 1, y], [x, y + 1], [x, y - 1]];
            for (const [nx, ny] of neighbors) {
                if (nx >= 0 && nx < grid[0].length && ny >= 0 && ny < grid.length &&
                    grid[ny][nx] !== '#' && !visited.has(`${nx},${ny}`)) {
                    visited.add(`${nx},${ny}`);
                    queue.push([nx, ny, dist + 1]);
                }
            }
        }
        
        distanceCache.set(key, Infinity);
        return Infinity;
    }

    const remainingJobs = [...jobs];

    while (remainingJobs.length > 0) {
        let bestJobIndex = -1;
        let bestRobotId = -1;
        let minTotalDist = Infinity;

        for (let i = 0; i < remainingJobs.length; i++) {
            const job = remainingJobs[i];
            
            for (const robotId of robotIds) {
                const dist = getDistance(robotPositions[robotId], job);
                if (dist < minTotalDist) {
                    minTotalDist = dist;
                    bestJobIndex = i;
                    bestRobotId = robotId;
                }
            }
        }

        if (bestJobIndex === -1) break; // Should not happen if jobs are reachable

        const job = remainingJobs[bestJobIndex];
        assignments[bestRobotId].push(job.id);
        robotPositions[bestRobotId] = { x: job.x, y: job.y };
        remainingJobs.splice(bestJobIndex, 1);
    }

    const result = {
        assignments: Array.from({ length: maxRobotId + 1 }, (_, i) => assignments[i] || [])
    };

    process.stdout.write(JSON.stringify(result));
}

solve();

Test 59: Drone Search Coverage Planner (JavaScript) Score: 4.56 / 6
Typical Prompt:
Write a complete Node.js JavaScript program for this benchmark task.

Plan grid paths for search drones.

The input JSON contains:
- `probability`: 2D array; -1 is an obstacle, positive values are search value
- `drones`: starting cells `{id, x, y}`
- `maxStepsPerDrone`

Return JSON:
```
{"paths": [[[x,y], [x,y], ...], ...]}
```
Each path belongs to the drone with the same index. Moves must be up/down/left/right
or stay in place, must stay inside the grid, and must avoid obstacles. The score is
unique probability covered compared with a greedy reference search.

**Input format:**
The program receives one JSON document on stdin. Use this pattern to read it:
```
const fs = require('fs'); const input = fs.readFileSync(0, 'utf8');
const data = JSON.parse(input);
```

**Output format:**
Print one JSON document to stdout. Do not print logs or explanatory text.

[2 drones, 55 steps] coverage 14845, reference 14473, score 1.000 (run 0.06s)

Drone Search Coverage Planner - 2 drones, 55 steps

View JavaScript code (1287 chars)
const fs = require('fs');
const input = fs.readFileSync(0, 'utf8').replace(/^\uFEFF/, '');
const data = JSON.parse(input);

const { probability, drones, maxStepsPerDrone } = data;
const height = probability.length;
const width = probability[0].length;
const visitedByAnyone = new Set();
const paths = [];

for (const drone of drones) {
  let { x, y } = drone;
  const path = [[x, y]];
  visitedByAnyone.add(`${x},${y}`);

  for (let step = 0; step < maxStepsPerDrone; step++) {
    let bestMove = { x, y };
    let maxScore = -1;

    const moves = [
      { dx: 0, dy: 0 },
      { dx: 0, dy: -1 },
      { dx: 0, dy: 1 },
      { dx: -1, dy: 0 },
      { dx: 1, dy: 0 }
    ];

    for (const move of moves) {
      const nx = x + move.dx;
      const ny = y + move.dy;

      if (nx >= 0 && nx < width && ny >= 0 && ny < height && probability[ny][nx] !== -1) {
        let score = 0;
        if (!visitedByAnyone.has(`${nx},${ny}`)) {
          score = probability[ny][nx];
        }
        if (score > maxScore) {
          maxScore = score;
          bestMove = { x: nx, y: ny };
        }
      }
    }

    x = bestMove.x;
    y = bestMove.y;
    path.push([x, y]);
    visitedByAnyone.add(`${x},${y}`);
  }
  paths.push(path);
}

process.stdout.write(JSON.stringify({ paths }));

Test 60: Space Mission Work Schedule (JavaScript) Score: 3.99 / 6
Typical Prompt:
Write a complete Node.js JavaScript program for this benchmark task.

Schedule mission-control work across parallel workstations.

The input JSON contains:
- `machineCount`
- `tasks`: objects `{id, duration, deps, deadline, weight}`

A task may start only after every dependency has finished. Return either:
```
{"schedule": [{"id": taskId, "start": integerTime, "machine": machineIndex}, ...]}
```
or a simpler topological order:
```
{"order": [taskId, taskId, ...]}
```
If you return only an order, the grader will place tasks greedily in that order.
The score compares weighted lateness plus makespan against a deterministic list
scheduler reference.

**Input format:**
The program receives one JSON document on stdin. Use this pattern to read it:
```
const fs = require('fs'); const input = fs.readFileSync(0, 'utf8');
const data = JSON.parse(input);
```

**Output format:**
Print one JSON document to stdout. Do not print logs or explanatory text.

**Runtime environment:**

Architecture: AMD64
CPU: Intel64 Family 6 Model 158 Stepping 10, GenuineIntel
Core count: 12
Memory: 31.93 GB

The grader will run the same program on increasingly large scenarios. Use algorithms
that scale; avoid browser APIs, network access, and external npm dependencies.

Parameters: (see typical prompt)View exact prompt

Schedule dependent mission tasks across parallel workstations with deadlines.		 0.6291
[24 tasks, 3 workstations] cost 582.3, makespan 106; reference 366.3, makespan 86; score 0.629 (run 0.06s)

Space Mission Work Schedule - 24 tasks, 3 workstations

View JavaScript code (1811 chars)
const fs = require('fs');

function solve() {
    let input = '';
    try {
        input = fs.readFileSync(0, 'utf8');
    } catch (e) {
        // Handle potential read error
        return;
    }
    
    if (!input) return;

    const data = JSON.parse(input);
    const { tasks } = data;

    const taskMap = new Map();
    for (const task of tasks) {
        taskMap.set(task.id, task);
    }

    const graph = new Map(); // dependencyId -> [taskId_that_depends_on_it]
    const inDegree = new Map(); // taskId -> count

    for (const task of tasks) {
        inDegree.set(task.id, task.deps.length);
        for (const depId of task.deps) {
            if (!graph.has(depId)) {
                graph.set(depId, []);
            }
            graph.get(depId).push(task.id);
        }
    }

    const readyQueue = [];
    for (const task of tasks) {
        if (inDegree.get(task.id) === 0) {
            readyQueue.push(task);
        }
    }

    const order = [];
    while (readyQueue.length > 0) {
        // Sort readyQueue by heuristic: lowest deadline, highest weight
        readyQueue.sort((a, b) => {
            const deadlineA = a.deadline ?? Infinity;
            const deadlineB = b.deadline ?? Infinity;
            if (deadlineA !== deadlineB) {
                return deadlineA - deadlineB;
            }
            return b.weight - a.weight;
        });

        const current = readyQueue.shift();
        order.push(current.id);

        const dependents = graph.get(current.id) || [];
        for (const depId of dependents) {
            inDegree.set(depId, inDegree.get(depId) - 1);
            if (inDegree.get(depId) === 0) {
                readyQueue.push(taskMap.get(depId));
            }
        }
    }

    process.stdout.write(JSON.stringify({ order }));
}

solve();

Typical Prompt:
Write a complete Node.js TypeScript program for this benchmark task.

Plan delivery routes for a fleet of vans.

The input JSON contains:
- `depot`: [x, y]
- `truckCount`: maximum number of vans/routes
- `packages`: objects `{id, x, y}`

Return JSON:
```
{"routes": [[packageId, packageId, ...], ...]}
```
Every package must appear exactly once. Each route starts and ends at the depot.
The score compares your total distance to a deterministic reference planner.

**Input format:**
The program receives one JSON document on stdin. Use this pattern to read it:
```
declare const require: any; const fs = require('fs'); const input: string = fs.readFileSync(0, 'utf8');
const data = JSON.parse(input);
```

**Output format:**

Parameters: (see typical prompt)View exact prompt

Plan multi-vehicle delivery routes over clustered city coordinates.		 1.0000
[30 packages, 2 vans] distance 2327.4, reference 3027.0, score 1.000 (run 0.06s)

Delivery Route Planner - 30 packages, 2 vans

View TypeScript code (1648 chars)
declare const require: any;
const fs = require('fs');

interface Package { id: number; x: number; y: number; }
interface Data { depot: [number, number]; truckCount: number; packages: Package[]; }

function dist(p1: [number, number], p2: [number, number]): number {
  return Math.sqrt(Math.pow(p1[0] - p2[0], 2) + Math.pow(p1[1] - p2[1], 2));
}

function solve() {
  const input: string = fs.readFileSync(0, 'utf8');
  const data: Data = JSON.parse(input);
  const { depot, truckCount, packages } = data;

  const routes: number[][] = Array.from({ length: truckCount }, () => []);
  const packageLocations: Map<number, [number, number]> = new Map();
  packages.forEach(p => packageLocations.set(p.id, [p.x, p.y]));

  for (const pkg of packages) {
    let bestInsertion = {
      routeIndex: -1,
      position: -1,
      cost: Infinity
    };

    for (let r = 0; r < truckCount; r++) {
      const route = routes[r];
      
      // Try inserting at all possible positions
      for (let pos = 0; pos <= route.length; pos++) {
        const prev = pos === 0 ? depot : packageLocations.get(route[pos - 1])!;
        const next = pos === route.length ? depot : packageLocations.get(route[pos])!;
        
        const oldDist = dist(prev, next);
        const newDist = dist(prev, [pkg.x, pkg.y]) + dist([pkg.x, pkg.y], next);
        const cost = newDist - oldDist;

        if (cost < bestInsertion.cost) {
          bestInsertion = { routeIndex: r, position: pos, cost };
        }
      }
    }

    routes[bestInsertion.routeIndex].splice(bestInsertion.position, 0, pkg.id);
  }

  process.stdout.write(JSON.stringify({ routes }));
}

solve();

Test 62: Wildfire Firebreak Planner (TypeScript) Score: 0.00 / 7
Typical Prompt:
Write a complete Node.js TypeScript program for this benchmark task.

Choose where to cut firebreaks before a wildfire spreads.

The input JSON contains:
- `width`, `height`
- `grid`: strings where `.` is burnable forest and `~` is water/rock
- `ignitions`: starting fire cells `[x, y]`
- `towns`: objects `{id, x, y, value}`
- `cutBudget`: maximum number of forest cells you may cut

Fire spreads in four directions through burnable cells. Water/rock and your cut
cells block fire. Return JSON:
```
{"firebreaks": [[x, y], [x, y], ...]}
```
The score is the fraction of otherwise-burned town value saved.

**Input format:**
The program receives one JSON document on stdin. Use this pattern to read it:
```
declare const require: any; const fs = require('fs'); const input: string = fs.readFileSync(0, 'utf8');
const data = JSON.parse(input);
```

**Output format:**
Print one JSON document to stdout. Do not print logs or explanatory text.

**Runtime environment:**

Architecture: AMD64
CPU: Intel64 Family 6 Model 158 Stepping 10, GenuineIntel
Core count: 12
Memory: 31.93 GB

SIMD detail: AVX=yes, AVX-512F=no, AVX2=yes, SSE=yes, SSE2=yes, SSE3=yes, SSE4.1=yes, SSE4.2=yes, SSSE3=yes
Node.js: v24.14.0

Return the complete runnable TypeScript source code.

Subpass 0
Parameters: (see typical prompt)View exact prompt
View raw AI output
View chain of thought
Choose firebreak cells on a forest map to protect towns from spreading fire.		 0.0000
[32x22 forest, 3 towns] town value burned 27/27, burned cells 655/663, valid cuts 8 (run 0.06s)

Wildfire Firebreak Planner - 32x22 forest, 3 towns

View TypeScript code (2404 chars)
declare const process: any;

interface Town {
  id: number;
  x: number;
  y: number;
  value: number;
}

interface InputData {
  width: number;
  height: number;
  grid: string[];
  ignitions: [number, number][];
  towns: Town[];
  cutBudget: number;
}

function processData(input: string) {
  // Remove BOM if present
  const cleanedInput = input.replace(/^\uFEFF/, '');
  const data: InputData = JSON.parse(cleanedInput);

  const { width, height, grid, ignitions, towns, cutBudget } = data;

  // BFS to find shortest path from any ignition to each cell
  const dist = Array.from({ length: height }, () => Array(width).fill(Infinity));
  const parent = Array.from({ length: height }, () => Array(width).fill(null));
  const queue: [number, number][] = [];

  for (const [x, y] of ignitions) {
    dist[y][x] = 0;
    queue.push([x, y]);
  }

  let head = 0;
  while (head < queue.length) {
    const [x, y] = queue[head++];
    const neighbors: [number, number][] = [
      [x + 1, y], [x - 1, y], [x, y + 1], [x, y - 1]
    ];

    for (const [nx, ny] of neighbors) {
      if (nx >= 0 && nx < width && ny >= 0 && ny < height && grid[ny][nx] === '.' && dist[ny][nx] === Infinity) {
        dist[ny][nx] = dist[y][x] + 1;
        parent[ny][nx] = [x, y];
        queue.push([nx, ny]);
      }
    }
  }

  // Identify vulnerable towns
  const vulnerableTowns = towns.filter(t => dist[t.y][t.x] !== Infinity);
  
  // For each vulnerable town, find the path back to an ignition
  const cellScores = new Map<string, number>();

  for (const town of vulnerableTowns) {
    let curr: [number, number] | null = [town.x, town.y];
    while (curr) {
      const [x, y] = curr as [number, number];
      // Don't cut the town itself (could be problematic) or ignitions (they are already on fire)
      if (grid[y][x] === '.' && dist[y][x] > 0) {
        const key = `${x},${y}`;
        cellScores.set(key, (cellScores.get(key) || 0) + town.value);
      }
      curr = parent[y][x];
    }
  }

  // Pick top N cells
  const sortedCells = Array.from(cellScores.entries())
    .sort((a, b) => b[1] - a[1])
    .slice(0, cutBudget);

  const firebreaks = sortedCells.map(c => c[0].split(',').map(Number) as [number, number]);

  process.stdout.write(JSON.stringify({ firebreaks }));
}

let input = '';
process.stdin.on('data', (chunk: any) => input += chunk);
process.stdin.on('end', () => processData(input));

Test 63: Warehouse Robot Job Planner (TypeScript) Score: 1.44 / 6
Typical Prompt:
Write a complete Node.js TypeScript program for this benchmark task.

Assign warehouse pick jobs to robots.

The input JSON contains:
- `grid`: strings where `.` is open floor and `#` is blocked shelving
- `robots`: objects `{id, x, y}`
- `jobs`: objects `{id, x, y, priority}`

Return JSON:
```
{"assignments": [[jobId, jobId, ...], ...]}
```
The array index is the robot id. Every job must appear exactly once. The grader
uses shortest grid walking distance through open cells and compares total travel
to a deterministic greedy reference.

**Input format:**
The program receives one JSON document on stdin. Use this pattern to read it:
```
declare const require: any; const fs = require('fs'); const input: string = fs.readFileSync(0, 'utf8');
const data = JSON.parse(input);
```

**Output format:**
Print one JSON document to stdout. Do not print logs or explanatory text.

**Runtime environment:**

Core count: 12
Memory: 31.93 GB

Parameters: (see typical prompt)View exact prompt

View chain of thought
Assign warehouse pick jobs to robots moving through aisles and blocked shelves.		 0.3434
[28 jobs, 2 robots] travel 399, reference 137, score 0.343 (run 0.05s)

Warehouse Robot Job Planner - 28 jobs, 2 robots

View TypeScript code (2308 chars)
declare const require: any;
declare const process: any;
const fs = require('fs');

interface Point {
    x: number;
    y: number;
}

interface Robot extends Point {
    id: number;
}

interface Job extends Point {
    id: number;
    priority: number;
}

interface Input {
    grid: string[];
    robots: Robot[];
    jobs: Job[];
}

function getDistances(start: Point, grid: string[]): number[][] {
    const height = grid.length;
    const width = grid[0].length;
    const distances: number[][] = Array.from({ length: height }, () => Array(width).fill(Infinity));
    
    const queue: Point[] = [start];
    distances[start.y][start.x] = 0;
    
    const directions = [
        { x: 0, y: 1 },
        { x: 0, y: -1 },
        { x: 1, y: 0 },
        { x: -1, y: 0 }
    ];
    
    while (queue.length > 0) {
        const { x, y } = queue.shift()!;
        
        for (const dir of directions) {
            const nx = x + dir.x;
            const ny = y + dir.y;
            
            if (nx >= 0 && nx < width && ny >= 0 && ny < height && grid[ny][nx] === '.' && distances[ny][nx] === Infinity) {
                distances[ny][nx] = distances[y][x] + 1;
                queue.push({ x: nx, y: ny });
            }
        }
    }
    
    return distances;
}

function solve() {
    let input: string = fs.readFileSync(0, 'utf8');
    // Strip BOM if present
    if (input.length > 0 && input.charCodeAt(0) === 0xFEFF) {
        input = input.slice(1);
    }
    const data: Input = JSON.parse(input);
    
    const robotDistances = data.robots.map(robot => getDistances(robot, data.grid));
    
    const maxRobotId = Math.max(...data.robots.map(r => r.id));
    const assignments: number[][] = Array.from({ length: maxRobotId + 1 }, () => []);
    
    for (const job of data.jobs) {
        let minDistance = Infinity;
        let bestRobotId = -1;
        
        for (const robot of data.robots) {
            const dist = robotDistances[robot.id][job.y][job.x];
            if (dist < minDistance) {
                minDistance = dist;
                bestRobotId = robot.id;
            }
        }
        
        if (bestRobotId !== -1) {
            assignments[bestRobotId].push(job.id);
        }
    }
    
    process.stdout.write(JSON.stringify({ assignments }));
}

solve();

Test 64: Drone Search Coverage Planner (TypeScript) Score: 4.56 / 6
Typical Prompt:
Write a complete Node.js TypeScript program for this benchmark task.

Plan grid paths for search drones.

The input JSON contains:
- `probability`: 2D array; -1 is an obstacle, positive values are search value
- `drones`: starting cells `{id, x, y}`
- `maxStepsPerDrone`

Return JSON:
```
{"paths": [[[x,y], [x,y], ...], ...]}
```
Each path belongs to the drone with the same index. Moves must be up/down/left/right
or stay in place, must stay inside the grid, and must avoid obstacles. The score is
unique probability covered compared with a greedy reference search.

**Input format:**
The program receives one JSON document on stdin. Use this pattern to read it:
```
declare const require: any; const fs = require('fs'); const input: string = fs.readFileSync(0, 'utf8');
const data = JSON.parse(input);
```

**Output format:**
Print one JSON document to stdout. Do not print logs or explanatory text.

**Runtime environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: Intel64 Family 6 Model 158 Stepping 10, GenuineIntel
Core count: 12
Memory: 31.93 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3
SIMD detail: AVX=yes, AVX-512F=no, AVX2=yes, SSE=yes, SSE2=yes, SSE3=yes, SSE4.1=yes, SSE4.2=yes, SSSE3=yes
Node.js: v24.14.0
TypeScript compiler: Version 6.0.3

The grader will run the same program on increasingly large scenarios. Use algorithms
that scale; avoid browser APIs, network access, and external npm dependencies.
Return the complete runnable TypeScript source code.
 Prompt Changes:
Prompt parameters change between subpasses (increasing difficulty)
Subpass 0
Parameters: (see typical prompt)View exact prompt
View raw AI output
View chain of thought
Route search drones over a probability heatmap with obstacles and battery limits.		 1.0000
[2 drones, 55 steps] coverage 14845, reference 14473, score 1.000 (run 0.05s)

Drone Search Coverage Planner - 2 drones, 55 steps

View TypeScript code (2463 chars)
declare const require: any;
const fs = require('fs');

interface Drone {
    id: number;
    x: number;
    y: number;
}

interface InputData {
    probability: number[][];
    drones: Drone[];
    maxStepsPerDrone: number;
}

function solve() {
    const input: string = fs.readFileSync(0, 'utf8');
    const data: InputData = JSON.parse(input);
    const { probability, drones, maxStepsPerDrone } = data;

    const rows = probability.length;
    const cols = probability[0].length;
    const covered: boolean[][] = Array.from({ length: rows }, () => Array(cols).fill(false));

    const paths: [number, number][][] = [];

    for (const drone of drones) {
        let cx = drone.x;
        let cy = drone.y;
        const path: [number, number][] = [[cx, cy]];

        // Mark starting position as covered if it's not an obstacle
        if (probability[cy][cx] !== -1) {
            covered[cy][cx] = true;
        }

        for (let step = 0; step < maxStepsPerDrone; step++) {
            let bestMove = { x: cx, y: cy, val: -1 };

            // Possible moves: stay, up, down, left, right
            const moves = [
                { dx: 0, dy: 0 },
                { dx: 0, dy: -1 },
                { dx: 0, dy: 1 },
                { dx: -1, dy: 0 },
                { dx: 1, dy: 0 }
            ];

            for (const move of moves) {
                const nx = cx + move.dx;
                const ny = cy + move.dy;

                // Check bounds and obstacle
                if (nx >= 0 && nx < cols && ny >= 0 && ny < rows && probability[ny][nx] !== -1) {
                    // Only count value if not yet covered
                    const val = covered[ny][nx] ? 0 : probability[ny][nx];
                    
                    // Choose the move with the highest potential value
                    if (val > bestMove.val) {
                        bestMove = { x: nx, y: ny, val };
                    } else if (bestMove.val === -1) {
                        // If all potential moves have 0 value, any valid move is fine
                        bestMove = { x: nx, y: ny, val };
                    }
                }
            }

            cx = bestMove.x;
            cy = bestMove.y;
            path.push([cx, cy]);
            if (probability[cy][cx] !== -1) {
                covered[cy][cx] = true;
            }
        }
        paths.push(path);
    }

    process.stdout.write(JSON.stringify({ paths }));
}

solve();

Test 65: Space Mission Work Schedule (TypeScript) Score: 4.00 / 6
Typical Prompt:
Write a complete Node.js TypeScript program for this benchmark task.

Schedule mission-control work across parallel workstations.

The input JSON contains:
- `machineCount`
- `tasks`: objects `{id, duration, deps, deadline, weight}`

A task may start only after every dependency has finished. Return either:
```
{"schedule": [{"id": taskId, "start": integerTime, "machine": machineIndex}, ...]}
```
or a simpler topological order:
```
{"order": [taskId, taskId, ...]}
```
If you return only an order, the grader will place tasks greedily in that order.
The score compares weighted lateness plus makespan against a deterministic list
scheduler reference.

**Input format:**
The program receives one JSON document on stdin. Use this pattern to read it:
```
declare const require: any; const fs = require('fs'); const input: string = fs.readFileSync(0, 'utf8');
const data = JSON.parse(input);
```

**Output format:**
Print one JSON document to stdout. Do not print logs or explanatory text.

**Runtime environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: Intel64 Family 6 Model 158 Stepping 10, GenuineIntel
Core count: 12
Memory: 31.93 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3
SIMD detail: AVX=yes, AVX-512F=no, AVX2=yes, SSE=yes, SSE2=yes, SSE3=yes, SSE4.1=yes, SSE4.2=yes, SSSE3=yes
Node.js: v24.14.0
TypeScript compiler: Version 6.0.3

The grader will run the same program on increasingly large scenarios. Use algorithms
that scale; avoid browser APIs, network access, and external npm dependencies.
Return the complete runnable TypeScript source code.

Subpass 0

View raw AI output
View chain of thought
Schedule dependent mission tasks across parallel workstations with deadlines.		 0.6487
[24 tasks, 3 workstations] cost 564.7, makespan 114; reference 366.3, makespan 86; score 0.649 (run 0.05s)

Space Mission Work Schedule - 24 tasks, 3 workstations

View TypeScript code (1706 chars)
const fs = require('fs');

interface Task {
    id: number;
    duration: number;
    deps: number[];
    deadline: number;
    weight: number;
}

interface Input {
    machineCount: number;
    tasks: Task[];
}

function solve() {
    let input: string;
    try {
        input = fs.readFileSync(0, 'utf8');
    } catch (e) {
        return;
    }
    
    if (!input) return;
    
    const data: Input = JSON.parse(input);

    // Topological Sort
    const taskMap = new Map<number, Task>();
    data.tasks.forEach(task => taskMap.set(task.id, task));

    const adj = new Map<number, number[]>(); // taskId -> dependents
    const inDegree = new Map<number, number>();

    data.tasks.forEach(task => {
        inDegree.set(task.id, task.deps.length);
        task.deps.forEach(dep => {
            if (!adj.has(dep)) adj.set(dep, []);
            adj.get(dep)!.push(task.id);
        });
    });

    const queue: number[] = [];
    data.tasks.forEach(task => {
        if (inDegree.get(task.id) === 0) {
            queue.push(task.id);
        }
    });

    const order: number[] = [];
    while (queue.length > 0) {
        // Greedy heuristic: prefer earlier deadline
        queue.sort((a, b) => {
            const ta = taskMap.get(a)!;
            const tb = taskMap.get(b)!;
            return ta.deadline - tb.deadline;
        });

        const u = queue.shift()!;
        order.push(u);

        const dependents = adj.get(u) || [];
        dependents.forEach(v => {
            inDegree.set(v, inDegree.get(v)! - 1);
            if (inDegree.get(v) === 0) {
                queue.push(v);
            }
        });
    }

    process.stdout.write(JSON.stringify({ order }));
}

solve();
